raw_output:
The technique you are referring to is known as Divide-and-Conquer, where a complex problem is broken down into simpler sub-problems of the same type until these become simple enough to be solved directly. The solutions to the sub-problems are then combined to give a solution to the original problem.

A classic example of this technique is the Quick Sort algorithm, which sorts an array by selecting a pivot element from the array and partitioning the other elements into two sub-arrays, according to whether they are less than or greater than the pivot. This process is then recursively applied to each sub-array until all elements are sorted.

While it's not explicit in my explanation, Quick Sort can indeed be implemented using recursion. However, it can also be implemented iteratively, demonstrating that Divide-and-Conquer doesn't necessarily involve recursion but instead relies on the fundamental concept of breaking down a problem into smaller instances.
